artifacts.content:
Subject: Catching you up on Fremont (and everything else)

Hi Lisa,

I’m hiding in the bedroom while Eli and Michael argue about whose turn it is to walk Jasper, so it feels like a good time to actually write you a real update instead of just texting.

Work first: Fremont Park is finally moving. We kept the scope really tight – straight rehab of the existing playground at 1515 Q Street, no expansion – so it fits neatly under the Class 1, 2, and 4 CEQA exemptions. After that anonymous HR complaint a neighbor filed on 10/3/22 accusing me of “pushing through playground projects without proper environmental review,” and the talk Mark pulled me into on 10/10/22 at the Engineering Services office, I’m honestly terrified of anything that looks remotely controversial. He basically said, “Make sure Fremont is textbook exempt and that the record spells out why we don’t need an EIR.”

It’s not just politics, it’s my sanity. After the panic attack I had at Fremont on 5/12/23 during Eli’s Sutter Middle soccer practice, Dr. Angela Morris at Sutter Health Midtown spent most of our June–August sessions telling me to avoid big, high‑conflict projects for a while. So I’ve been very intentional about keeping Fremont a clean, in‑kind replacement: new poured‑in‑place rubber, accessible routes, shade, lighting tweaks. Nothing that would trigger hearings or litigation.

On the home front, Dad’s Parkinson’s is about the same. Mom’s still stubborn about letting me drive her to Kaiser South Sac, but she gives in if I bribe her with farmers’ market peaches. Rachel’s buried in AP World and college TikToks; Eli’s mostly soccer and Minecraft.

How are your kiddos and your OT caseload? I keep thinking about that little guy from the 16th & P complex who melts down on the noisy metal slide. I really am trying to make Fremont better for kids like him.

Love,
Debby

metadata.subject: Catching you up on Fremont (and everything else)

artifacts.content:
Title: Fremont Park – personal reflections & design reminders

I keep circling back to why this little 1.5‑acre park at 1515 Q Street matters so much to me.

1. Eli’s fall – 2/18/23
On February 18, 2023, Eli tripped on that broken concrete edge where the old playground surfacing meets the sidewalk on the 16th Street side. We ended up at Sutter Medical Center for four stitches. He was brave, but I was furious at that stupid lip of concrete. The new poured‑in‑place rubber and smoother accessible pathway absolutely have to eliminate that kind of trip hazard.

2. Mom’s knee recovery – May 2022
In May 2022, when Mom was recovering from her knee replacement at Kaiser South Sacramento, I tried to take her to Fremont. Watching her struggle with the uneven turf and lack of handrails just to reach a bench near the play area was awful. She had her walker and still couldn’t get comfortable. That’s burned into my brain any time someone suggests “minimal” accessibility.

3. Rachel’s safety text – 10/3/23
Rachel’s text from October 3, 2023, around 5:47 p.m., is another anchor: “Mom, can you pick me up at Fremont? The corner by Q feels sketchy. Lights are super dim and some guys were yelling at us.” That’s exactly the northeast corner where the lighting is terrible.

Design mantra: continuous rubber surfacing, real accessible routes and seating for people with walkers/canes, and upgraded, energy‑efficient lighting that makes teens feel safe staying past 5:30 p.m. without turning the place into a stadium.

Remember: this isn’t abstract. It’s Eli’s stitches, Mom’s walker, and Rachel’s texts.

metadata.title: Fremont Park – personal reflections & design reminders

artifacts.content:
Subject: Money stress + why I’m clinging to “easy” projects

Hey Lisa,

Quick vent while I wait for a Teams meeting to start.

You know about the small claims mess, but I don’t think I told you how much it’s coloring work decisions. On 2/7/23 the Sacramento County Superior Court entered that $3,850 judgment against us for the unpaid ER bills from Rachel’s UC Davis Medical Center visit. Michael’s been pretty blunt that I cannot afford to be the PM who overruns budgets right now – not with college looming and that debt hanging over us.

So when Fremont Park came up, I was determined to keep it as a low‑cost, exempt rehab. Class 1, 2, and 4 only – replacement of the old playground equipment, rubberized surfacing, minor landscaping, upgraded LED lighting on existing poles, and swapping the grimy drinking fountain for an accessible bottle‑filling station. No expansion, no new buildings, nothing that would force us into a full CEQA review or outside consultants.

That lines up with what Mark told me after the anonymous HR complaint last October accusing me of cutting corners on environmental review. Our 10/10/22 chat in his office was basically, “Your next park project needs to be bulletproof on exemptions.” I took that to heart.

Add in Dr. Morris’s advice after my panic attack at Fremont on 5/12/23 – she was very clear in our June–August sessions at Sutter Midtown that I should avoid high‑conflict, litigation‑magnet projects – and the conservative approach pretty much chose itself.

Anyway, that’s my glamorous life: juggling CEQA classes, medical bills, and teenagers’ homework. How’s Oakland treating you this week?

xo,
Debby

metadata.subject: Money stress + why I’m clinging to “easy” projects

artifacts.content:
Subject: Fremont lighting + your OT kiddos

Hi Lisa,

I was thinking about our March 2023 conversation while reviewing the latest Fremont Park playground plans this morning. You mentioned that pediatric OT client from the affordable housing complex at 16th & P who completely melts down on the current noisy metal slide at Fremont. I can still hear you describing how the sound and vibration send him into sensory overload, and how you’ve had to document those triggers in your treatment notes.

We’re using that as a design cue. The new layout replaces the loud metal slide with quieter composite materials and adds inclusive sensory panels and rubber surfacing that should dampen noise. I’ve been telling the design team, “Think about Lisa’s client – we want kids like him to be able to stay and play, not run away.”

At the same time, I can’t ignore the broader safety vibe. Remember Rachel’s text on 10/3/23 around 5:47 p.m. from the northeast corner by Q Street – “Mom, can you pick me up at Fremont? The corner by Q feels sketchy. Lights are super dim and some guys were yelling at us.” That’s exactly why we’re upgrading the security lighting on the existing poles and adding the small shade structure: better visibility and comfort for teens hanging out after school.

We’re also swapping the old fountain for a bottle‑filling station. A bunch of folks downtown still talk about that reported needle‑stick incident near the fountain back in November 2022. In my informal staff wellness survey on 6/12/23, several Engineering Services coworkers said they avoid Fremont at lunch because of the encampments along the 16th Street edge and that needle story.

So: quieter for your sensory‑sensitive kiddos, and less “sketchy” for my teen and my coworkers. That’s the goal.

Love,
Debby

Title: Work journal – HR complaint fallout & overdose memory

10/11/2022

Yesterday’s meeting with Mark is still sitting heavy. HR got an anonymous complaint from a neighbor on 10/3/22 accusing me of “pushing through playground projects without proper environmental review.” Mark called me into his office at the Engineering Services Division and did the gentle‑but‑serious supervisor talk. His main point: from here on out, any park work I touch – including the Fremont Park upgrade – needs to fit clearly within CEQA Class 1, 2, and 4 exemptions, and the file has to spell out exactly why no EIR is needed. No gray areas.

Part of me is defensive; I know the guidelines cold. But part of me also understands the optics, especially with Fremont. That park already has a complicated emotional history for me.

I keep flashing back to July 19, 2021, when the homeless man overdosed in the Fremont restroom. Rachel’s C.K. McClatchy summer workout group was still in the park, and I ended up giving a statement to Sacramento Police at the scene. Watching officers and EMTs work while trying to keep an eye on Rachel’s group was surreal. Since then, I’ve been wary of anything that might invite more late‑night activity there.

So, for my own sanity, I want the Fremont project to stay modest: better lighting on existing poles, improved sightlines from the benches, rubberized surfacing, accessible paths, minor landscaping. No new facilities that could turn into another flashpoint or drag us into a big CEQA fight.

Note to self: document everything clearly, but don’t let the fear of another complaint paralyze me. The whole point is safer, more inclusive public space – for kids, for people living outside, for everyone.

metadata.title: Work journal – HR complaint fallout & overdose memory

artifacts.content:
Title: Sunday check‑in with Mom & Dad + Fremont walk

Date: 2023-09-09
Time: 10:00 a.m. – 2:30 p.m.
Location: Start at Elk Grove (parents’ house), then Fremont Park, 1515 Q Street, Sacramento
Attendees: Debby, Ruth, David, possibly Michael and kids

Plan:

10:00 a.m. – Pick up peaches, tomatoes, and greens at Midtown farmers’ market.
11:00 a.m. – Drive to Elk Grove. Help Mom sort meds and set up pill organizer for the week. Check in on Dad’s physical therapy exercises and how he’s doing with the Parkinson’s meds since the March 2022 diagnosis at Kaiser South Sacramento.

12:30 p.m. – Load everyone in the car and head to Fremont Park. Goal is to walk the approximate route from parking to the existing benches and playground so I can see, in real time, what Dad’s “about 200 feet” walking tolerance looks like now. Bring tape measure and notebook.

At the park, pay attention to:
- How uneven turf and missing accessible routes affect Mom with her lingering knee issues from the May 2022 surgery at Kaiser South Sac.
- Where Dad needs to pause or lean, and how far the benches are from the sidewalk.
- The broken concrete edge where Eli tripped on 2/18/23 and ended up needing stitches at Sutter Medical Center.

Use this walk‑through to sanity‑check that the Fremont rehab stays within Class 1 and 2 CEQA exemptions while still delivering truly accessible, in‑kind upgrades (rubberized surfacing, ADA‑compliant paths, better seating). No scope creep.

2:30 p.m. – Drop parents back home, quick grocery unload, head home to start dinner and supervise homework.

metadata.title: Sunday check‑in with Mom & Dad + Fremont walk

artifacts.content:
Subject: Quick debrief on Fremont staff survey + my own nerves

Hi Mark,

Wanted to follow up on the Fremont Park scope after our last check‑in.

As you know, we’ve kept the project tightly within the Class 1, 2, and 4 CEQA exemptions: rehabilitation and minor alteration of the existing playground and amenities at Fremont Park, 1515 Q Street. We’re talking replacement of 20‑year‑old equipment in the same footprint, new poured‑in‑place rubberized surfacing, accessible concrete pathways tying into Q and 16th, upgraded LED lighting on existing poles, minor landscaping, and swapping the drinking fountain for an accessible bottle‑filling station. No new buildings, no expansion of use.

The informal staff wellness survey I ran on 6/12/23 confirmed what we’ve heard anecdotally: several Engineering Services folks avoid Fremont at lunch because of recurring encampments along the 16th Street edge and the reported needle‑stick incident near the existing fountain in November 2022. That feedback reinforces the value of the lighting upgrades and fountain replacement, but I’m being careful not to frame the project as “encampment removal.” It’s about safety and usability for everyone.

On a personal note – and I’m sharing this because it affects how I manage risk – the panic attack I had at Fremont on 5/12/23 during Eli’s Sutter Middle soccer practice shook me more than I admitted at the time. My therapist, Dr. Angela Morris at Sutter Health Midtown, has been pretty clear in our June–August sessions that I should avoid taking on highly contentious projects or anything likely to escalate into litigation‑level CEQA battles.

All of that is why I’ve structured Fremont as a straightforward, well‑documented exempt project with a strong record. Happy to walk you through the draft NOE language when you have time.

Best,
Debby

metadata.subject: Quick debrief on Fremont staff survey + my own nerves
